items.q: What is the difference between reusing and recycling?
items.correct: Reuse keeps the product or component intact and uses it again; recycling destroys the product to reprocess its material into new feedstock, which consumes energy.
items.misconception: Reusing and recycling are two words for the same process.
items.random_wrong: Recycling means selling a product second-hand.
items.why: The terms blur because both divert waste. Reuse preserves the embodied energy of the whole product, which is why it ranks above recycling in the hierarchy.

items.q: Is recycling cost-free for the environment?
items.correct: No - collection, transport, sorting and reprocessing all consume energy and can create emissions; recycling is beneficial but not impact-free.
items.misconception: Yes - once material is recycled, its environmental impact is zero.
items.random_wrong: Yes - recycling generates energy rather than using it.
items.why: The green image hides the logistics. Recycling usually beats virgin production, but reduction and reuse avoid the processing chain altogether.

items.q: Is treating pollution after production the best way to manage it?
items.correct: No - clean technology aims to prevent pollution at source, which is generally cheaper and more effective than end-of-pipe treatment afterwards.
items.misconception: Yes - filtering emissions at the chimney is the ideal solution.
items.random_wrong: Yes - pollution can only ever be dealt with after it is created.
items.why: End-of-pipe fixes are visible and familiar. Prevention redesigns the process itself, eliminating the pollutant stream rather than perpetually capturing it.

items.q: In the waste hierarchy, the most effective strategy is to:
items.correct: Reduce the resources consumed in the first place
items.misconception: Recycle as much material as possible
items.why: It's Reduce the resources consumed in the first place. “Recycle as much material as possible” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Design for disassembly makes a product more sustainable because it:
items.correct: Allows easy separation of parts for repair, reuse and material recovery
items.misconception: Makes the product impossible to open
items.random_wrong: Increases the number of glued joints
items.why: Correct answer: Allows easy separation of parts for repair, reuse and material recovery. “Makes the product impossible to open” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which is the best example of dematerialisation?
items.correct: Replacing printed manuals with a downloadable digital version
items.misconception: Adding extra packaging layers for luxury appeal
items.random_wrong: Using thicker steel than the design requires
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Replacing printed manuals with a downloadable digital version. “Adding extra packaging layers for luxury appeal” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Clean technology differs from end-of-pipe treatment because it:
items.correct: Prevents pollution and waste from being created at source
items.misconception: Filters pollution after it leaves the factory
items.why: It's Prevents pollution and waste from being created at source. “Filters pollution after it leaves the factory” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: A product uses 0.5 kg of plastic. A redesign cuts material use by 20%. How many kilograms are saved across 1,000 units?
items.why: Exact answer: 100 kg. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
